data.title: Tracking Ray Dalio's Bridgewater Associates 13F Portfolio - Q1 2025 Update
data.description:
This article is part of a series that provides an ongoing analysis of the changes made to Bridgewater Associates’ 13F portfolio on a quarterly basis. It is based on their regulatory 13F Form filed on 05/14/2025. Please visit our Tracking Ray Dalio’s Bridgewater Associates 13F Portfolio series to get an idea of their investment philosophy and our previous update for the fund’s moves during Q4 2024.

Assets Under Management is at around $155B. The SPDR Bridgewater ALL Weather ETF (NASDAQ:ALLW) is a way to get exposure to Bridgewater for the retail audience. The 13F portfolio is less than ~15% of their total AUM. This quarter, the 13F portfolio value decreased from ~$21.81B to ~$21.55B. The holdings are diversified with recent reports showing around 1000 different stakes. Around 39 of them are significantly large (more than ~0.5% of the 13F portfolio) and they are the focus of this article. The top three individual stock positions are at ~7% while the top five holdings are close to ~10% of the 13F assets: Alibaba Group Holdings, Alphabet, Booking Holdings, Nvidia, and Microsoft.

Note 1: The firm uses asset class diversification among uncorrelated positions to achieve absolute returns. As such the stakes can be on or against debt, equity, and other markets around the world. To learn more about their unique investment philosophy, check out Ray Dalio’s books.

Note 2: Ray Dalio is a Sinophile whose fascination for China goes back to 1984.

Stake Increases:

Alibaba Group Holding (BABA): BABA is now the largest position in the portfolio at 3.47%. It was primarily built this quarter at prices between ~$80 and ~$148. The stock currently trades at ~$117.

Booking Holdings (BKNG): The top-three 1.43% of the portfolio position was built this quarter at prices between ~$4279 and ~$5282. The stock is now well above that range at ~$5502.

Microsoft Corp (MSFT): MSFT is a 1.41% of the portfolio position that saw a ~90% stake increase during Q2 2024 at prices between ~$388 and ~$452. The position was sold down by ~20% in the next quarter at prices between ~$394 and ~$467. The last quarter saw a ~23% selling at prices between ~$406 and ~$455 while this quarter there was a ~21% increase at prices between ~$367 and ~$448. The stock is now at ~$459.

Salesforce Inc. (CRM): CRM is a 2.30% of the portfolio position that saw a stake doubling this quarter at prices between ~$259 and ~$367. The stock currently trades near the bottom of that range at ~$267.

Fiserv Inc. (FI): The 1.19% FI stake saw a ~80% stake increase this quarter at prices between ~$200 and ~$239. The last quarter also saw a ~20% stake increase at prices between ~$179 and ~$223. The stock is now well below those ranges at ~$160.

Amazon.com (AMZN): The 1.17% of the portfolio AMZN position was established during Q1 2024 at prices between ~$145 and ~$180. The next quarter saw a ~150% stake increase at prices between ~$174 and ~$198. There was a ~47% selling during Q3 2024 at prices between ~$161 and ~$200. That was followed by a one-third reduction during the last quarter at prices between ~$181 and ~$233. There was a ~44% stake increase this quarter at prices between ~$184 and ~$243. The stock currently trades at ~$206.

Wells Fargo (WFC): WFC is a 1.14% of the portfolio position that saw a ~75% stake increase this quarter at prices between $65.52 and $81.50. The stock is now at $74.51.

Visa Inc. (V): The 1.13% Visa stake saw a ~18% increase during Q1 2023 at prices between ~$207 and ~$231. Q3 2023 saw a ~16% trimming while in the next quarter there was a ~28% stake increase at prices between ~$229 and ~$262. The stake was decreased by 13% during Q1 2024 at prices between ~$257 and ~$290. The next quarter saw another ~15% selling at prices between ~$262 and ~$280. Q3 2024 saw another ~25% reduction at prices between ~$253 and ~$291. This quarter saw a ~14% stake increase at prices between ~$304 and ~$367. The stock is now at ~$362.

PayPal Holdings (PYPL): PYPL is now a 1.09% of the portfolio position. The stake was almost doubled during the last quarter at prices between ~$77 and ~$91. That was followed by a ~50% stake increase this quarter at prices between ~$63 and ~$93. The stock is now at ~$71.

Adobe Inc. (ADBE): The ~1% ADBE position saw a ~150% stake increase this quarter at prices between ~$375 and ~$466. The stock currently trades at ~$413.

Advanced Micro Devices (AMD): AMD is a ~1% stake established during Q1 2024 at prices between ~$135 and ~$211. There was a ~62% stake increase during the next quarter at prices between ~$144 and ~$183. Q3 2024 saw a one-third stake increase at prices between ~$129 and ~$184. There was a ~23% stake increase during the last quarter at prices between ~$119 and ~$173. This quarter also saw a similar increase at prices between ~$95 and ~$132. The stock is now at ~$113.

Apple Inc. (AAPL): The ~1% AAPL stake was more than doubled during Q3 2024 at prices between ~$207 and ~$234. The last quarter saw a ~40% selling at prices between ~$221 and ~$259 while this quarter there was a ~52% increase at prices between ~$208 and ~$250. The stock is now at ~$200.

Qualcomm Inc. (QCOM): QCOM is a 0.78% of the portfolio position built during Q3 2024 at prices between ~$155 and ~$209. There was a ~13% stake increase during the last quarter at prices between ~$150 and ~$177. That was followed by a ~20% stake increase this quarter at prices between ~$148 and ~$177. The stock currently trades at ~$148.

Broadcom (AVGO): AVGO is a 0.73% of the portfolio position built during Q3 2024 at prices between ~$136 and ~$178. There was a ~31% reduction during the last quarter at prices between ~$159 and ~$249 while in this quarter there was a ~43% increase at prices between ~$161 and ~$250. The stock is now at ~$242.

Altria Group (MO), Baidu Inc. (BIDU), Bristol-Myers Squibb (BMY), Comcast Corp (CMCSA), Expedia Group (EXPE), Fortinet Inc. (FTNT), GE Vernova (GEV), MetLife (MET), Citigroup (C), Palo Alto Networks (PANW), PDD Holdings (PDD), and ServiceNow (NOW): These stakes were increased this quarter.

Stake Decreases:

Alphabet Inc. (GOOG): GOOG is currently the second largest 13F individual stock position at 2.18% of the portfolio. It saw a ~210% stake increase during Q1 2023 at prices between ~$87 and ~$109. There was a ~15% trimming in the next quarter and that was followed by a ~20% reduction during Q3 2023 at prices between ~$117 and ~$139. Q4 2023 saw a ~30% stake increase at prices between ~$123 and ~$143. The position was increased by 163% during Q1 2024 at prices between~ $133 and $~155. There was a ~15% trimming during the next quarter at prices between ~$152 and ~$187. That was followed by a ~17% selling during the last quarter at prices between $162.88 and $198.16. This quarter also saw a similar reduction at prices between ~$152 and ~$209. The stock currently trades at ~$173.

Nvidia Corp. (NVDA): The top five 1.43% NVDA position was increased by 162% during Q1 2024 at prices between ~$48 and ~$95. The stake was established in Q4 2023 at prices between ~$40 and ~$51. Q3 2024 saw a ~28% selling at prices between $98.90 and $134.90. That was followed by a ~26% reduction during the last quarter at prices between $116.99 and $148.87. This quarter also saw a ~19% selling at prices between ~$104 and ~$153. The stock currently trades at ~$139.

Meta Platforms (META): META is a 1.14% of the portfolio stake that was increased by 49% during Q1 2024 at prices between ~$344 and ~$512. Q3 2024 saw a ~17% reduction at prices between ~$453 and ~$568. That was followed by a ~23% selling during the last quarter at prices between $553.63 and $632.17. This quarter also saw a ~32% reduction at prices between ~$553 and ~$741. The stock is now at ~$645.

Vistra Corp (VST): The 0.89% VST stake saw a ~135% increase during Q3 2024 at prices between ~$69 and ~$119. The last quarter saw a ~60% further increase at prices between ~$116 and ~$166. The stock is now at ~$160. There was a ~8% trimming this quarter.

Constellation Energy (CEG): The 0.88% CEG position saw a ~23% stake increase during the last quarter at prices between ~$224 and ~$285. The stock currently trades at ~$303. This quarter saw marginal trimming.

Lam Research (LRCX): The 0.66% LRCX stake was primarily built during Q3 2024 at prices between ~$71 and ~$109. This quarter saw a ~23% selling at prices between ~$70 and ~$92. The stock currently trades at ~$84.

McKesson Corp (MCK): MCK is a 0.49% stake that saw a one-third increase during the last quarter at prices between ~$482 and ~$628. This quarter saw a ~53% selling at prices between ~$566 and ~$679. The stock currently trades at ~$713.

Walmart Stores (WMT): The WMT position was purchased during the four quarters through Q2 2021 at prices between ~$40 and ~$51. Q4 2021 saw the stake sold down by ~45% at prices between ~$45 and ~$50. H1 2022 had seen a two-thirds increase at prices between ~$39 and ~$53. The four quarters through Q2 2023 saw a ~48% selling at prices between ~$41 and ~$52. There was a ~55% reduction during Q3 2024 at prices between ~$67 and ~$81. The last quarter saw a one-third selling at prices between ~$79 and ~$96. This quarter saw the position almost sold out at prices between ~$84 and ~$105. The stock is now at ~$97.

Note: The prices quoted above are adjusted for the 3-for-1 stock split in February 2024.

AppLovin Corp (APP), AT&T Inc. (T), HCA Healthcare (HCA), Merck Inc. (MRK), and The Cigna Group (CI): These stakes were reduced during the quarter.

The spreadsheet below highlights changes to Bridgewater Associates’ 13F holdings during Q1 2025:

Source: John Vincent. Data constructed from Bridgewater Associates’ 13F filings for Q4 2024 and Q1 2025.

data.title: COWG: High Tech, High Momentum, High Returns, High Valuation
data.description:
US large-cap growth has rebounded into positive territory year-to-date. While mega-cap tech took it on the chin to begin the year, a May rebirth among the Magnificent Seven stocks has helped send both the Nasdaq 100 ETF (QQQ) and the S&P 500 Growth ETF (SPYG) back to black for 2025.

One new fund, the Pacer US Large Cap Cash Cows Growth Leaders ETF (NASDAQ:COWG) has tracked US large-cap growth since its inception less than three years ago. The fund received a boost, including an influx of investor flows, following the US election in 2024. I noticed big volume over the past handful of months, but I have a hold rating on the product.

The valuation is not all that compelling, while its risky holdings make COWG susceptible to a pullback as we approach mid-year. I’ll call out key price levels to monitor at the conclusion of the article.

COWG Very Strong Post-Election, Returns On Pace with SPYG

According to the issuer, COWG is a strategy-driven ETF that aims to identify top growth companies in the Russell 1000 by screening for above-average free cash flow margins. Free cash flow is the cash remaining after a company has paid expenses, interest, taxes, and long-term investments. It is the source from which dividends are paid. Companies that have the highest free cash flow margin over time may produce higher current and future sales and earnings growth.

Ranked No. 3 out of 28 in its Sub Class, COWG is a small ETF with just $1.8 billion in assets under management as of May 28, 2025. Its annual expense ratio is moderate at 49 basis points, which is about 0.4 percentage points above what you can get for a standard US growth fund. Moreover, the trailing 12-month dividend yield is low at only 31 basis points, which is about a full percentage point below that of the S&P 500.

Share-price momentum has been extremely strong lately, though, as evidenced by a pristine A+ ETF Grade in that category by Seeking Alpha’s quantitative scoring system. COWG has benefited from a superb rebound in the US equity momentum factor, and you’ll see later that some of its biggest components are momentum darlings.

But COWG sports just average risk grades, and we’ve already seen this year that steep corrections can happen on a moment’s notice. From a liquidity perspective, there aren’t many issues, considering its average daily volume of more than 700,000 shares and a median 30-day bid/ask spread of less than two basis points, per Pacer ETFs.

Looking closer at the portfolio, what makes me uneasy with COWG today is the valuation. Now trading at nearly 25x earnings, the PEG ratio is almost three, given the long-term earnings growth rate of only 9%. Performance depends on key themes playing out, including AI and cybersecurity. While I am bullish on those fronts, and I appreciate the profitability and free cash flow focuses, the ETF is not a bargain today.

COWG: Portfolio & Factor Profiles

Increasing the product’s risk is the portfolio breakdown. More than 60% of COWG is classified as being in the Information Technology sector, with another 13% in Health Care. That’s about three-quarters of the allocation in just two growth-oriented areas. So, there’s a lack of industry diversification.

COWG: Holdings & Dividend Information

From a seasonal point of view, I must concede that bullish winds are blowing. We typically see US large-cap growth perform quite well now through July. Though COWG includes a significant chunk of mid-cap US growth, we can still use SPYG calendar trends as a decent proxy for how the ETF may perform if seasonal price action is any clue.

US Growth Tends to Perform Well Through July-August

For COWG’s holdings specifically, I’d call out that we’ll get a handful of Annual General Meeting events now through the middle of June. Five of its top 10 company holdings have shareholder events through June 18, so that could stir some share-price volatility before the Q2 earnings season gets underway.

Shareholder Meetings on Tap

The Technical Take

From a technical perspective, COWG looks solid. Notice in the chart below that we’ve seen a massive volume increase just since January. The fund performed incredibly well after the election, and that alpha period may have drawn in new money. Shares peaked, though, in mid-February, and then went on to plunge about 30% to the April low, sharply underperforming the S&P 500.

I like what I see in the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the graph, but key price resistance remains in the $35 to $36 zone, so we’ll see if the bulls can bring the growth ETF through that level during this typically strong seasonal stretch. I do see a downside gap around the $33 mark that could be tagged in the weeks ahead, but with a high amount of volume by price down to $32, we might just be in an ongoing congestion zone.

Bigger picture, the long-term 200-day moving average remains positively sloped, suggesting that the bulls control the primary trend.

COWG: Strong Uptrend, Big Volume YTD, $36 Resistance, $33 Support

The Bottom Line

I have a hold rating on COWG. The growth-oriented US equity fund that seems to have a bent to the momentum factor has a premium valuation, while its chart is constructive heading into the summer.

data.title: AAII Sentiment Survey: Pessimism Makes A Comeback
data.description:
Pessimism among individual investors about the short-term outlook for stocks increased in the latest AAII Sentiment Survey. Meanwhile, optimism and neutral sentiment decreased.

Bullish sentiment, expectations that stock prices will rise over the next six months, decreased 4.8 percentage points to 32.9%. Bullish sentiment is below its historical average of 37.5% for the 16th time in 17 weeks, and is above 30% for only the eighth time this year.

Neutral sentiment, expectations that stock prices will stay essentially unchanged over the next six months, decreased 0.3 percentage points to 25.2%. Neutral sentiment is below its historical average of 31.5% for the 45th time in 47 weeks.

Bearish sentiment, expectations that stock prices will fall over the next six months, increased 5.1 percentage points to 41.9%. Bearish sentiment is unusually high and is above its historical average of 31.0% for the 26th time in 28 weeks.

The bull-bear spread (bullish minus bearish sentiment) decreased 10.0 percentage points to –9.0%. The bull-bear spread is below its historical average of 6.5% for the 21st time in 23 weeks.

This week’s special question asked AAII members which factor is most influencing their six-month outlook for stocks.

Here is how they responded:

  • Tariffs, the economy, and/or inflation: 63.7%
  • Corporate earnings: 10.7%
  • Valuations: 10.4%
  • Monetary policy/interest rates: 8.9%
  • Other: 6.3%

This week’s Sentiment Survey results:

  • Bullish: 32.9%, down 4.8 percentage points
  • Neutral: 25.2%, down 0.3 percentage points
  • Bearish: 41.9%, up 5.1 percentage points

Historical averages:

  • Bullish: 37.5%
  • Neutral: 31.5%
  • Bearish: 31.0%

The AAII Sentiment Survey has been conducted weekly since July 1987.

data.title: BND: When Diversification Becomes Diworsification
data.description:
Diversification is protection against ignorance. It makes little sense if you know what you are doing. - Warren Buffett

Peter Lynch famously coined the term "diworsification" to describe situations where spreading an investment too thin did not help reduce risks much, but did affect performance by adding subpar investments into the mix. The Vanguard Total Bond Market Index Fund ETF (NASDAQ:BND) is a great example, holding over 10,000 bonds, it basically guarantees mediocre returns. While it does have some advantages, like an extremely low expense ratio, it has also barely delivered any real returns since it was launched.

A large portion of the portfolio is in U.S. government or government-backed securities (Treasuries and agency mortgage-backed securities), with the remainder in high-grade corporate bonds. This means credit risk is low, but the ETF does have significant interest rate risk, as its effective average duration is almost six years. This means BND will likely face significant headwinds if interest rates continue to rise due to high inflation or elevated fiscal deficits. In other words, high inflation and rising yields are a significant risk for BND.

Historical Performance

Compared to the major categories of high-quality U.S. bonds, BND has underperformed all of them, a hint that "diworsification" could be at fault. It has even delivered lower total returns than the iShares 7-10 Year Treasury Bond ETF (IEF), whose average duration is relatively close. The biggest gap has been with the iShares Investment Grade Corporate Bond ETF (LQD), although in this case it could be argued that higher returns were to be expected to compensate for the added credit risk. The iShares TIPS Bond ETF (TIP) and the iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond ETF (TLT) also delivered higher total returns, though these two ETFs carry higher duration risk.

Perhaps more concerning, BND's total return has barely outpaced the US Consumer Price Index. In other words, real returns have been less than 1%, not to mention the enormous underperformance compared to other defensive assets such as gold (GLD).

Portfolio Characteristics

BND provides exposure to the entire U.S. investment-grade bond market (Treasuries, agency mortgage-backed securities, and investment-grade corporate bonds) in one fund. Probably its two biggest selling points are the extremely low expense ratio of only 0.03% and the high liquidity given its massive assets under management (AUM). The BND ETF excludes high-yield “junk” bonds and emerging markets debt. This keeps credit risk low but also means BND’s income might lag funds that take more credit risk.

BND’s yield to worst is currently around 4.5%, the highest it has been in over a decade, thanks to the rise in interest rates. This yield roughly approximates the fund’s expected long-term return should yields stay at current levels. The average duration of ~5.8 years puts it firmly in the intermediate duration category.

The U.S. Dollar Factor

For an international investor, purchasing BND means investing in USD-denominated bonds, and therefore taking on currency exposure, unless they hedge it. The problem is that currency hedging can be expensive and often results in net yields close to the local-currency government bonds. For example, at the moment Japanese investors would have to pay around 4% annually to hedge their currency risk, resulting in final yields close to what long-term Japanese government debt offers. At the moment the U.S. trade-weighted dollar index is trading above its ten-year average, hinting at potential overvaluation.

Alternatives

While BND certainly offers a very simple approach to gain broad-market bond exposure at a low cost, we see some potential alternatives without taking significantly more risk.

For example, a combination of medium-term Treasuries, with IEF, and a small percentage in corporate bonds, with LQD, could result in higher total returns. A more tailored approach could be done depending on the risks that one wants to mitigate. For example, the Vanguard Short-term Inflation Protected ETF (VTIP) has a short interest rate duration, inflation protection, and excellent credit quality. Investors looking to avoid duration risk could consider options such as the iShares 0-3 Month Treasury Bond ETF (SGOV).

For those seeking an almost identical option from another provider, the iShares Core U.S. Aggregate Bond ETF (AGG) offers a very similar ETF, at basically the same cost, and with basically the same advantages and drawbacks. A more intriguing option is probably the Vanguard Total International Bond ETF (BNDX), which seeks broad exposure across major bond markets outside of the United States, and uses hedging strategies to mitigate exchange rate risk. Its expense ratio is slightly higher at 0.07%, but its yield-to-worst is currently a more attractive 4.9%.

Future Outlook

The current macroeconomic backdrop includes relatively high inflation, although below the peak reached in 2022, coupled with elevated fiscal deficits. There is also risk that inflation could be reignited by tariffs and increased fiscal deficits. On the positive side for BND investors, yields are the highest in years, implying better forward returns. This higher starting yield means future returns should be stronger than what investors experienced over the last decade of ultra-low rates.

For BND, an environment of persistent inflation and tight Fed policy could mean the ~4.5% yield could be offset by price losses if yields creep upward. If the U.S. fiscal deficit and debt issuance are large, this could keep long-term interest rates higher than they’d otherwise be, which would limit BND’s near-term upside or result in price declines.

Risks

When interest rates rise BND’s price usually declines to compensate. This rate-driven risk resulted in significant losses in 2022 as inflation overshot expectations, forcing the Fed to increase interest rates from near-zero to more than 4%. BND similarly tends to underperform in scenarios of rising government deficits and increased supply of bonds, which can steepen the yield curve and lift long-term rates. BND also has some corporate bonds, which adds some non-negligible credit risk. Still, BND tends to be significantly less volatile than assets like gold, which in recent decades experienced a draw-down almost twice the worst experienced by BND.

Takeaway

The BND ETF has shown that it is a low-cost, low-risk, and easy way to gain broad exposure to the bond market. Unfortunately, it has also shown that it offers low-rewards, with its total real returns barely in positive territory. As Buffett quipped, diversification is protection against ignorance. This means it could be a decent option for an investor that does not want to think too hard about fixed income.

Still, with BND underperforming most U.S. investment-grade bond categories, it feels less like smart diversification and more like the "diworsification" Peter Lynch warned us about. Investors are paying almost nothing in fees, but they are also not earning much in real terms. In a world where inflation is sticky and fiscal deficits are high, "owning everything" might not be such a great idea.

data.title: LVMH: Problems Mount Inside The Empire
data.description:
LVMH (OTCPK:LVMHF) shares are in a 45% drawdown, as the company suffers from declining demand for its once unattainable products. Meanwhile, scarcity-focused Hermes (OTCPK:HESAF) is not so far from its all-time highs.

As a result, for the first time in I don’t know how many years, Hermes surpassed LVMH to become the world’s highest valued luxury company. And that is quite symbolic.

What is going on? Is the LVMH empire falling or are we at an attractive entry point? Let’s find out.

Introduction

I’ve been covering LVMH on Seeking Alpha for a little over two years now. Back in 2023, luxury companies, including LVMH, have demonstrated extreme resiliency during a time almost all other sectors were declining in light of rising inflation, extreme valuations, and the burst of the ZIRP demand boom.

Initially, I was bullish on LVMH, expecting them to continue showing resiliency and growing. Then, around mid-2024, I changed my tone.

The stock is now down about 45% from its post-pandemic peaks. In my view, the reasons for the drawdown are in line with what I’ve warned back in my September article. In short, there are three big problems LVMH is dealing with.

One, its extraordinary growth during 2022-2023 benefitted meaningfully from aspirational customers. These customers aren’t that wealthy, but they had a lot of money during ZIRP. Once the macro deteriorated, they left, and LVMH doesn’t have enough “core” luxury demand to replace them.

Two, the core luxury customer wants scarcity. They want to buy clothes and accessories that make them feel elevated above the middle class. Once LVMH reached immense scale, it lost desirability in the eyes of said customers, who are the ones that should support the resiliency of the business on a year-to-year basis.

I’ll add that from my point of view, “loud” luxury in general seems to be going out of trend these days.

Three, operationally, costs got meaningfully higher, and there isn’t much more room to raise prices in light of the weaker demand and after the aggressive price increased during the highs of 2022-2023. Meanwhile, inventory levels are yet to normalize.

All these problems are clearly reflected in LVMH’s results.

First Quarter Numbers Erase Hopes For Recovery On Easier Comps

As LVMH shares started falling, the main debate was about whether the decline in growth is a matter of tougher comps, or is it a structural drop in demand. Initially, I was in the first camp, but I was quick and early to change my mind.

Last quarter, put a final blow to the tougher comps argument. LVMH is now fully lapping negative growth quarters with more negative growth.

Wines & Spirits have seen negative organic growth in 7 of the last 9 quarters, and the decline is worsening. Fashion & Leather has five consecutive quarter of organic growth lower than 2%, and three consecutive negative quarters. Perfumes & Cosmetics turned negative last quarter, while Watches & Jewelry zeroed on negative comps. Even Selective Retailing, which was the bright spot led by Sephora, turned negative in the first quarter.

Looking at geographies, Asia has seen double-digit declines for 4 consecutive quarters, Japan and the US turned negative, and Europe has seen low-single digit growth since Q4-23.

The problem for a business like LVMH, is that it has very significant fixed costs, which are quite difficult to cut, especially as an immediate reaction. As a result, the declines in sales are dragging down margins, taking LVMH almost all the way back to pre-pandemic levels.

So, all in all, there’s really nothing to get excited about here, and nothing to truly rely on as a signal for improvement. In fact, LVMH itself recently made investors aware that the worst is likely yet to come.

LVMH Is Not In A Kering-Level Crisis, But It’s Definitely Losing Its Dominant Position

For years, LVMH was considered second only to Hermes in luxury fashion. Investors grew to expect meaningful outperformance over peers like Kering (OTCPK:PPRUF) or Richemont (OTCPK:CFRHF), with consistent market share gains.

Well, this is no longer the case.

We can see LVMH losing ground to Richemont in recent quarters, something that investors couldn't have imagined just a few years ago, when the gap was so significant in the opposite direction that takeover rumors surfaced.

Typically, when businesses that are based on brand are losing their appeal, it take a very long time to climb out of that hole. LVMH is not that deep in the mud just yet, but the signs are concerning.

Valuation & Prospects

When a company like LVMH is in such a significant drawdown, the first instinct from investors is to go ahead and buy shares, blindly assuming that things will improve. One of the main themes in my articles covering such companies, the type that have a long history of being high quality long-term compounders but are now struggling, is to warn investors from blindly assuming a recovery.

Things don’t improve out of nowhere, and usually, there are signs before a turnaround starts materializing. In my mind, no signs have been shown with regards to LVMH.

In fact, we’re hearing more and more about internal issues, including CEO changes in certain units, and layoffs. Although it shows LVMH is trying to turn things around, there’s still long ways to go.

So, at EUR 483 a share, or $553 per LVMHF ADR, the stock is supposedly cheap compared to historical levels, but not so much relative to its near-term prospects.

The current valuation reflects a low-twenties multiple based on estimates that don't fully reflect the expected weakness this year. Also, when LVMH was an industry leader, there was good justification for the high-twenties multiples. That is no longer the case, as peers like Prada (OTCPK:PRDSY), Richemont, and others, are growing much faster.

So, to me, despite the drop, LVMH isn’t so obviously attractive just yet.

Conclusion

LVMH is in the midst of a borderline crisis, as it goes through the worst growth stretch in its history. This time, it's happening while some of its peers are doing far better.

The extraordinary growth in the post-pandemic boom is coming back to bite the luxury empire, which is dealing with declining desirability, weakening brand image, and a very tough macro environment.

Investors' first instinct is to go ahead and buy such significant dips in a historically-amazing company, but I'd encourage patience, as there is no sign whatsoever for a turnaround in the near term.

As such, I reiterate LVMH at a 'Hold'.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Fat Dividends Served Reality Check
data.description:
We’re posting charts for price-to-book using the Q1 2025 book values. However, we're forecasting some fairly significant changes in book value for the mortgage REITs so far during Q1 2025. Are those changes increasing or decreasing book value? Silly optimist. Of course the changes are reducing book value.

Why do you care? Because the book value represents the amount management has available to invest. When a mortgage REIT or BDC has more book value (or net asset value), it's easier to produce income. We’re constantly working on providing new updated estimates in The REIT Forum. Now you might suggest that management simply work harder to produce income. Yeah, might as well tell them to produce some unicorns. Not happening. This is simply about playing the odds.

As usual, we’ll have a bunch of charts near the end of the article.

The Big Story

Two Harbors got slapped around lately. They have notably underperformed all agency mortgage REIT peers in the last two months, last month, and last two weeks.

I addressed the weakness in our weekly update for subscribers. I’ll paraphrase it here:

“TWO’s management did a bad job during the pandemic. The board felt they could terminate the management contract for cause, removing the requirement to pay a substantial amount of money to the external manager. That resulted in a legal battle spanning several years. It appears Two Harbors will lose. In this case, a loss for Two Harbors would be a loss for shareholders as it would allow the former external manager to collect a substantial amount of money. Who doesn’t love a story where the executive who participated in overseeing the huge loss on book value in the company wins a 9 figure payday? Possibly around $140 million, maybe a bit more. Remember, we have a legal system, not a justice system.

Note: Technically, the winner would be the external manager where the executive is or was a shareholder. After all, corporations are "people" and it isn't technically the executive winning. It is the corporation he owns / owned.

This loss is not currently factored into book value for Two Harbors. It may show up as early as Q2 2025, or it may show up later. However, given the likelihood of the loss, the risk rating and price target ranges for Two Harbors have been adjusted.”

Why do we say that it appears they will lose the case? Because that’s our impression from reading the court documents. Why else would we say it?

Why is Book Value Down?

Volatility. Mortgage REITs don’t like volatility. We’ve had the whole tariff thing. Moves in bond markets. They prefer low volatility.

How hard is book value getting slammed? Well, it depends on the mortgage REIT. We have a total of one where we estimate book value is up, but the gain is under 1%. Quite a bit under 1%. OK, it’s a rounding error. The ones that are getting smacked are down hard. We can’t say when TWO would recognize the hit, so I’m going to exclude them from the following statement. The rest of the mortgage REITs have projected declines in book value per share so far during Q2 2025 of 1.4% to 14.5%. Yes, that is a big range. They have materially different portfolios. When you model out different portfolios, you get different results.

OK, I'll mention one explicitly. ARMOUR Residential REIT is among the bigger projected losers. That isn't entirely surprising. They've managed to lose book values in all kinds of situations.

What about BDCs? Book value looks very steady. By our estimates the BDCs with the biggest BV gains are up about 1% and the biggest drops are about 2%. This is based on quarter-to-date (as of last Friday) changes comparing our estimates for 5/23/2025 with the end of Q1 2025. We calculate the changes for each stock individually, but this is just the big picture.

What Do I Like?

Pizza. Dogs. Snarky articles.

I’m still more interested in the lower-risk shares. I don’t like volatility in the underlying value. I want underlying values to be relatively stable so we can capitalize on a change in valuation. Despite that, I still have some positions in higher risk shares. How has that been working out? Not as well as desired. Thanks for asking.

I also like feedback from readers. Is there a chart you would like to see added to this series? Let me know about it.

I’ll go a step further. If you have any ideas about “the best thing you could add to this series,” post it in the comments. Most of the big improvements to this series are the result of reader suggestions. It sure wasn’t my creativity. I didn’t even come up with the idea for the charts. That was a reader suggestion. It used to be a table.

All The Stocks

The charts compare the following companies and their preferred shares or baby bonds:

  • BDCs: (CSWC), (BXSL), (TSLX), (OCSL), (GAIN), (TPVG), (FSK), (MAIN), (ARCC), (GBDC), (OBDC), (SLRC)
  • Commercial mREITs: (GPMT), (FBRT), (BXMT)
  • Residential Hybrid mREITs: (MITT), (CIM), (RC), (MFA), (EFC), (NYMT)
  • Residential Agency mREITs: (NLY), (AGNC), (CHMI), (DX), (TWO), (ARR), (ORC)
  • Residential Originator and Servicer mREITs: (RITM), (PMT)

Embedded Charts

Mortgage REITs and BDCs:

Preferred shares and baby bonds:

Thanks for reading and I hope you enjoyed the charts.

Some terminology:

  • FTF = Fixed-to-floating. Share is currently fixed but will begin floating based on SOFR. We may reference LIBOR, but that's assumed to be SOFR + 0.26161%.
  • FTR = Fixed-to-reset. Share is currently fixed. It will eventually begin resetting every five years based on the five-year Treasury rate.
  • FTL = Fixed-to-lawsuit. The company decided that their FTF shares could be "fixed-to-fixed" despite clearly violating the original intent of the contract.
  • Floating = A share that was FTF, but is now floating. The dividend rate is updated every three months.

Build a foundation for steady income with REITs

As demand for key real estate sectors increases and supply fails to keep pace, 2025 presents a prime opportunity to invest in REITs, Preferreds, and BDCs.

The REIT Forum offers a proven, transparent approach with actionable insights. Led by Colorado Wealth Management Fund (aka Michael VanLoon) and an expert team, subscribers receive precise trade alerts, portfolio tracking, and exclusive articles. Strategies from The REIT Forum have consistently outperformed sector indexes for nearly a decade.

Start your risk-free two-week trial today and capitalize on the power of real estate returns.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
